stem: An urn contains [[r]] red balls and [[b]] blue balls. A handful of [[k]] balls is drawn at random all at once (without replacement). What is the probability that exactly [[j]] of the drawn balls are red?
solution: Choose which [[j]] of the [[r]] red balls appear and which \( [[k]] - [[j]] \) of the [[b]] blue balls appear, out of all ways to choose [[k]] balls: \( \frac{\binom{[[r]]}{[[j]]}\binom{[[b]]}{[[k]] - [[j]]}}{\binom{[[r]] + [[b]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong denominator, choosing [[j]] balls from the whole urn instead of [[k]].
This forgets to count the ways of choosing the blue balls.
This chooses [[k]] red balls instead of [[j]] in the numerator.
This swaps the roles of the red and blue balls.
This is the naive fraction of the draw that is red, ignoring the counting.
This treats the draws as independent with replacement, so it is not the hypergeometric probability.

stem: A bag contains [[R]] red, [[G]] green, and [[Bl]] blue marbles. A handful of [[k]] marbles is drawn at random all at once (without replacement). What is the probability that the handful contains exactly [[r]] red, exactly [[g]] green, and exactly 2 blue marbles?
solution: Count favorable ways to pick each color separately and divide by all ways to pick [[k]] marbles from the whole bag: \( \frac{\binom{[[R]]}{[[r]]}\binom{[[G]]}{[[g]]}\binom{[[Bl]]}{2}}{\binom{[[R]]+[[G]]+[[Bl]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides the numerator by itself, collapsing to 1 rather than normalizing by the total number of ways to draw [[k]] marbles.
This adds the per-color counts instead of multiplying them.
This inverts the correct ratio.
This uses the wrong denominator, choosing only \( [[r]]+[[g]] \) marbles from the whole bag instead of all [[k]].
This (incorrectly) treats the three colors as if drawn independently with replacement.
This forgets to include the blue-marble count in the numerator.

stem: A committee of [[k]] people is chosen at random (without replacement) from a group of [[n]] people. Among the group, [[m]] particular people are close friends. What is the probability that ALL [[m]] of these particular people end up on the committee?
solution: Fix the [[m]] particular people as already on the committee, and choose the remaining \( [[k]]-[[m]] \) seats from the other \( [[n]]-[[m]] \) people, out of all ways to fill the committee: \( \frac{\binom{[[n]]-[[m]]}{[[k]]-[[m]]}}{\binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This (incorrectly) treats each of the [[m]] people's inclusion as an independent event with probability \( k/n \).
This forgets to reduce the number of remaining seats to fill by [[m]].
This forgets to reduce the pool of remaining people to choose from.
This is just the fraction of the group made up of the [[m]] particular people, ignoring the committee-selection structure.
This has the numerator and denominator's roles reversed.
This uses the wrong denominator, excluding the [[m]] particular people from the total count as well.

stem: A committee of [[k]] people is chosen at random (without replacement) from a group of [[n]] people that includes two particular rivals, Alice and Bob. What is the probability that AT LEAST ONE of Alice or Bob is left OFF the committee?
solution: The complement is "both Alice and Bob are on the committee," with probability \( \frac{\binom{[[n]]-2}{[[k]]-2}}{\binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}} \). So \( P(\text{at least one left off}) = 1 - \frac{\binom{[[n]]-2}{[[k]]-2}}{\binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the complement event (both are ON the committee), not the requested probability.
This forgets to reduce the number of seats to fill by 2 when conditioning on both particular people.
This wrongly complements a probability that itself forgot to reduce the seat count.
This treats it as a simple 2-out-of-n chance, ignoring the committee-selection structure.
This (incorrectly) treats each person's inclusion as an independent event with probability \( k/n \).
This uses the wrong denominator, excluding Alice and Bob from the total pool as well.

stem: A deck of \( [[R]] + [[B]] \) cards ([[R]] aces and [[B]] non-aces) is shuffled into a uniformly random order and dealt out one at a time, without replacement. What is the probability that the FIRST ace appears on draw number [[k]]?
solution: By symmetry, the position of the first ace has \( P(\text{first ace at draw } [[k]]) = \frac{\binom{[[B]]}{[[k]]-1}}{\binom{[[B]]+[[R]]}{[[k]]-1}} \cdot \frac{[[R]]}{[[B]]+[[R]]-[[k]]+1} = [[answer]] \): the first \( [[k]]-1 \) draws must all be non-aces, and then draw [[k]] must be an ace.
distractor_rationales: This treats all [[k]] draws as non-aces (using \( \binom{B}{k} \)) instead of only the first \( [[k]]-1 \), forgetting the final ace draw.
This is just the overall fraction of aces in the deck, ignoring the ordering requirement entirely.
This omits the final factor for drawing an ace exactly on draw [[k]], leaving only the "first \( [[k]]-1 \) are non-aces" part.
This wrongly repeats the SAME conditional probability for each of the \( [[k]]-1 \) prior draws instead of using combinatorial counting.
This uses the wrong denominator, choosing [[k]] positions for non-aces from the whole deck instead of \( [[k]]-1 \).
This is an unrelated ratio of remaining non-aces to remaining cards, not the waiting-time probability.

stem: A batch of [[n]] manufactured items contains [[d]] defective items. An inspector selects [[k]] items at random (without replacement) to test. What is the probability that NONE of the tested items is defective?
solution: Choose all [[k]] tested items from the \( [[n]] - [[d]] \) non-defective items, out of all ways to choose [[k]] items: \( \frac{\binom{[[n]] - [[d]]}{[[k]]}}{\binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the wrong denominator, choosing [[k]] items from the whole batch of [[n]] as if only [[d]] items existed to choose among.
This (incorrectly) treats each draw as independent with replacement, raising a single-draw probability to the [[k]]-th power.
This is the complement (at least one defective), not the probability of none being defective.
This chooses among the DEFECTIVE items instead of the non-defective ones.
This subtracts [[d]] from the number chosen as well as from the pool, an unnecessary extra correction.
This is an unrelated linear ratio rather than the correct combinatorial probability.
